fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to ship the TypeScript/JS code formatter plugin. Quick summary of what we need: it should take messy source code and spit out clean, consistently styled output. Users have been complaining that their code looks different across machines and PRs are full of noisy whitespace diffs — super frustrating for the team. The formatter needs to handle all the usual stuff: spacing, indentation, string quotes, semicolons, trailing commas, that kind of thing. Should also support JSX and the CJS module variant (check how we handled dialect detection in the file-type logic we already spec'd out). One thing that came up in the last retro — people want to be able to drop a special comment to opt out of formatting for a specific node or the whole file, similar to what we did for the ignore patterns in that other plugin. Also needs to be robust: bad input or weird config values shouldn't crash anything, they should come back as a clean error message. The whole thing needs to be split into proper modules — no giant single-file dumps please, the last time someone did that it was a nightmare to maintain. Oh and make sure running the formatter twice doesn't change anything the second time around. Ping me if anything's unclear but I think the test cases cover most of the edge cases we care about.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: format(sourceText: string, fileName: string, config: FormatterConfig): FormatterResult

trigger_keywords: what does the error output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error output must follow the exact key=value format over two lines: first line is `error=<error_type>` and second line is `detail=<message>`. For unparseable source the error_type is `parse_error` and the detail is `could not parse source text`. For unrecognized config values the error_type is `invalid_config` and the detail is `Found invalid value '<value>'.` — note the period at the end and the single quotes around the value.

There are two ignore directives. A whole-file ignore is triggered by the comment `// dprint-ignore-file` (with or without a space after `//`) appearing as the first comment in the file — the entire file is returned verbatim with no reformatting and no trailing newline is added. A per-node ignore is triggered by `// dprint-ignore` immediately preceding a statement — only that single node is preserved verbatim while the rest of the file is still formatted normally.

The dialect is selected by the `file_name` field in the request. TypeScript (`.ts`) is the default when no file name is provided. JSX syntax is accepted in plain JavaScript (`.js`) and CommonJS module (`.cjs`) files. The file name does not affect formatting rules — it only controls which parser dialect is used to accept or reject syntax.

A leading UTF-8 byte-order mark (U+FEFF) must be stripped from the input before formatting. The formatted output should never contain a leading BOM. The rest of the source is formatted normally after stripping.

Import sorting is controlled by the `module.sortImportDeclarations` config option. The only supported value is `caseSensitive`, which sorts import declarations by their module specifier string using raw code-point (byte-level) ordering. This means uppercase letters sort before lowercase. Imports are reordered as complete declaration lines — the bindings inside each import are not reordered.

Trailing comma behavior is configured per-construct. For array literals the config key is `arrayExpression.trailingCommas`. The three modes are: `never` removes any trailing comma regardless of layout; `always` ensures a trailing comma after the last element even on a single line; `onlyMultiLine` adds a trailing comma only when the literal is rendered across multiple lines and omits it when it fits on one line. The trailing-comma decision is made after the layout engine determines whether the construct wraps.

When `useTabs: true` is set, indentation characters are tab (`\t`) rather than spaces. The `indentWidth` option tells the layout engine how many columns a single tab represents, which affects line-width calculations for wrapping decisions. Wrapped continuation lines (e.g. a binary expression split across lines) are each indented with a single tab character. Block body indentation also uses tabs when this option is active.

trigger_keywords: when to keep parens on arrow
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `arrowFunction.useParentheses` option has two values. `force` always wraps the parameter list in parentheses, even for a lone identifier. `preferNone` drops parentheses only when there is exactly one parameter that is a bare untyped identifier — if the parameter has a TypeScript type annotation or if there are zero or multiple parameters, parentheses are kept. The option key uses camelCase dot notation: `arrowFunction.useParentheses`.

The `quoteProps` option has three modes. `asNeeded` strips quotes from any property name that is a valid JS identifier and preserves quotes on names that are not (e.g. names starting with a digit like `2bar`). `consistent` inspects all property names in a single object literal: if any one of them requires quotes, all names are quoted; otherwise all quotes are stripped. `preserve` leaves every property name's quoting exactly as written in the source.

The implementation must NOT be a single file. At minimum, the following concerns must be in separate modules: the parser front-end, the configuration model, the layout/printing engine, and the per-construct generation rules. The execution adapter (stdin/stdout + JSON decode/encode) must be its own module and must be the only place that touches I/O. The core formatting entry point must accept source text, a file name, and a config value — it must not read from stdin or write to stdout directly.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled dialect detection in the file-type logic we already spec'd out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 13 in the PRD and `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature13_file_type_detection.json` — the `file_name` field on the request selects the parser dialect (.ts default, .js and .cjs accept JSX)

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did for the ignore patterns in that other plugin
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 in the PRD covering `// dprint-ignore-file` (whole-file) and `// dprint-ignore` (per-node) directives, tested in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature10_1_ignore_file.json` and `feature10_2_ignore_node.json`
The `error=<type>\ndetail=<message>\n` two-line key=value format specified in Feature 14, tested in `feature14_1_parse_error.json` and `feature14_2_invalid_config.json`

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that point from the current requirements. Please check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature and refer to the PRD sections for that construct — those are the ground truth for expected behavior. If it's still unclear, implement the most conservative/strict interpretation and we can adjust after seeing the test results.

feature10_1_ignore_file.json.description: A whole-file ignore directive as the first comment causes the entire file to be returned verbatim with no reformatting.

feature10_2_ignore_node.json.description: A node-level ignore directive immediately preceding a statement preserves that node's original formatting while the rest of the file is still formatted.

feature11_bom_stripping.json.description: A leading byte-order mark is stripped from the output while the remaining source is formatted normally.

feature12_import_sorting.json.description: Import declarations are reordered by their module specifier; case-sensitive ordering sorts by raw code point.

feature13_file_type_detection.json.description: The file name's extension selects the dialect: JSX syntax is accepted in plain JavaScript and CommonJS module files.

feature14_1_parse_error.json.description: Source that cannot be parsed yields a language-neutral error record rather than any formatted output.

cases.expected_output:
error=parse_error
detail=could not parse source text

error=parse_error
detail=could not parse source text

feature14_2_invalid_config.json.description: An unrecognized configuration value yields a language-neutral error record identifying it as an invalid configuration.

feature1_default_whitespace.json.description: Format source with default options: collapse redundant spaces around operators, separators and statement terminators while leaving already-tidy code untouched.

feature2_1_indent_spaces.json.description: Block bodies are re-indented using spaces; the indent width is four columns by default.

feature2_2_indent_tabs.json.description: When tab indentation is enabled, wrapped/continued lines are indented with tab characters sized to the configured indent width.

input.text: const test = testingThisOutt + testtt + testingThisOut + testingthi + outt;

feature3_1_quote_always_double.json.description: With the always-double quote style, single-quoted string literals are converted to double quotes, escaping embedded double quotes as needed.

feature3_2_quote_always_single.json.description: With the always-single quote style, double-quoted string literals are converted to single quotes, escaping embedded single quotes as needed.

feature3_3_quote_prefer_double.json.description: The prefer-double quote style uses double quotes by default but keeps the original quoting when switching would require additional escaping.

feature3_4_quote_prefer_single.json.description: The prefer-single quote style uses single quotes by default but keeps the original quoting when switching would require additional escaping.

feature4_quote_props.json.description: Property-name quoting is controlled by a mode: as-needed removes quotes from identifier-like names, consistent quotes every name in an object if any one of them requires quotes, and preserve keeps the original quoting.

feature5_semicolons.json.description: Statement terminators follow a mode: always appends semicolons, while automatic-insertion mode omits them and only inserts one where required to avoid a parsing hazard.

feature6_trailing_commas.json.description: Trailing commas in array and object literals follow a mode: never removes them, always keeps one after the final element (including single-line), and only-multi-line adds one solely when the literal is broken across lines.

feature7_arrow_parentheses.json.description: Parentheses around a single arrow-function parameter follow a mode: force always wraps the parameter, while prefer-none drops parentheses for a lone untyped identifier parameter but keeps them when the parameter is typed or absent.

feature8_line_width_wrapping.json.description: When a construct exceeds the configured line width it is broken across multiple indented lines; a call whose arguments fit stays on one line.

input.text: const t = [other, other, other, otherTest, other];

cases.expected_output:
const t = [
    other,
    other,
    other,
    otherTest,
    other,
];

feature9_idempotent.json.description: Source that is already in canonical form is returned byte-for-byte unchanged.
